J1.basis: "this SaaS mid-pivoted from **Lumina** (AI meeting assistant) to **InboxZap** (inbox cleanup tool)" ... "the blog was fetching from an API that doesn't exist — while GSC showed the exact demand InboxZap should be capturing (bulk delete, gmail storage, unsubscribe, inbox zero)"
J1.rationale: The entrant inverted the pivot: it treats the inbox-cleanup queries as the CURRENT product's target market and the meeting assistant as the legacy product, then builds the entire plan around ranking InboxZap-era content harder — matching anchor_0 almost verbatim.

J2.basis: "Replaced with 12 server-rendered posts (/blog/post-1…post-12, matching the URLs GSC already has indexed) each targeting a specific underperforming query cluster... 'gmail storage full' – 790 impressions at position 43.6, 'how to reach inbox zero' – 1180 impressions at position 41.2"
J2.rationale: It cites real GSC impressions/positions, which earns minimal grounding credit, but there is no per-asset harvest/redirect/keep/sunset disposition — every legacy post is uniformly rebuilt and doubled down on rather than dispositioned against the data.

J3.basis: "Blog rebuilt... 12 server-rendered posts... each targeting a specific underperforming query cluster from gsc/Queries.csv" and "Homepage rewritten for the actual product... now match InboxZap"
J3.rationale: The entrant falls fully into one failure mode — doubling down on inbox-cleanup content as the growth engine — with no tension articulated and no transition/redirect plan; it never balances legacy equity against a new positioning.

J1.basis: this SaaS mid-pivoted from **Lumina** (AI meeting assistant) to **InboxZap** (inbox cleanup tool)... GSC showed the exact demand InboxZap should be capturing (bulk delete, gmail storage, unsubscribe, inbox zero)
J1.rationale: The entrant inverted the pivot: it read the meeting-assistant homepage as stale and the inbox-cleanup title/GSC as the current product, then built the entire plan around ranking InboxZap-era content harder — precisely the anchor_0 failure, just arrived at via a mistaken pivot direction rather than not noticing one.

J2.basis: Replaced with 12 server-rendered posts... each targeting a specific underperforming query cluster from `gsc/Queries.csv` (e.g. "gmail storage full" – 790 impressions at position 43.6...)
J2.rationale: No harvest/redirect/sunset dispositions are made for the legacy inventory; the GSC numbers are cited only to justify rebuilding and doubling down on inbox-cleanup content, which is the opposite of a migration plan for legacy assets.

J3.basis: The core problem... GSC showed the exact demand InboxZap should be capturing... sitting on indexed pages ranking at position 40–60 with near-zero clicks... Blog rebuilt... targeting a specific underperforming query cluster
J3.rationale: The entrant lands squarely in the first failure mode — doubling down on inbox-cleanup content for the demand it wrongly treats as current — with no articulation of the tension and no bridge to the meeting-assistant positioning.

J1.basis: this SaaS mid-pivoted from **Lumina** (AI meeting assistant) to **InboxZap** (inbox cleanup tool)
J1.rationale: The entrant explicitly names the pivot and frames every fix around the InboxZap reality, but inverts the direction the panel expects (treats meeting-assistant as legacy and inbox-cleanup as current) — from a practicality lens it commits fully to one coherent product reality and executes against it, though it doesn't treat inbox-cleanup as legacy demand.

J2.basis: 12 server-rendered posts (`/blog/post-1`…`post-12`, matching the URLs GSC already has indexed) each targeting a specific underperforming query cluster from `gsc/Queries.csv` (e.g. "gmail storage full" – 790 impressions at position 43.6
J2.rationale: It rebuilds all 12 posts against real GSC impression/position numbers and maps each to a query cluster, which is concrete and executable, but it applies one blanket disposition (rewrite-all) rather than per-asset harvest/redirect/keep/sunset decisions grounded in each post's individual data.

J3.basis: GSC showed the exact demand InboxZap should be capturing (bulk delete, gmail storage, unsubscribe, inbox zero) sitting on indexed pages ranking at position 40–60 with near-zero clicks
J3.rationale: It preserves indexed URLs and builds content against the demand rather than torching equity, so it avoids blind abandonment — but it never articulates the legacy-vs-current tension or sequences a transition, and (per its inverted read) leans fully into the inbox-cleanup queries as the current market rather than balancing a bridge.
